map_desc: Every confirmed detection with a known location, plotted locally on your device. Nothing here is shared with anyone yet — see the roadmap on the About page.
map_empty: No located detections yet — enable geolocation in Settings on the Live page and record a few sessions.
msg_geo_hint: Filters species based on your current location.
msg_threshold_hint: Minimum confidence score required to show a detection.
msg_rarity_guard_hint: Raises the threshold for species rarely seen here (based on location/season), to cut false positives on out-of-range birds. Set to 0 to disable. Each detection card also has its own +/− to override this per species.
msg_history_empty: No detections logged yet this session.
msg_sensitivity_hint: Higher sensitivity detects more birds but may increase false positives.
msg_inference_interval_hint: How often the model analyzes audio. Lower values are more responsive but use more battery.
msg_mic_gain_hint: Digital volume boost. Use if your microphone is too quiet.
msg_rumble_hint: Removes wind and traffic noise below this frequency.
msg_duration_hint: Length of the spectrogram history shown on screen.
msg_colormap_hint: Color scheme used for the spectrogram visualization.
msg_freq_range_hint: Visible frequency range. Most bird calls are between 1kHz and 10kHz.
msg_amp_range_hint: Visible loudness range. Adjust to increase contrast.
msg_explore_geo_disabled: Enable geolocation in Settings to see local species.
about_subtitle: Real-time bird sound identification for Sochi and the Black Sea coast, running entirely in your web browser.
about_demo_p1: Sochi Birds is a Progressive Web App (PWA) that runs the BirdNET model entirely inside your browser using TensorFlow.js — no app install, no server, no audio ever leaves your device.
about_demo_p2: It's built on top of the official BirdNET Live project (MIT licensed) from Cornell/Chemnitz, tuned for one fixed region instead of the whole planet: a per-species confidence threshold you can adjust individually, a rarity penalty that raises the bar for species rarely seen on this coast, and a running session history, which stock BirdNET apps don't keep at all.
about_urban_title: Why frame this as urban research?
about_urban_p1: Sochi is a useful test case: a narrow subtropical strip squeezed between the sea and the Caucasus foothills, under some of the fastest resort/residential construction pressure in Russia. Birdsong is a cheap, continuous, passive signal of ecological health that a planner or architect doesn't otherwise get — closer to a noise map or a heat map than to a birdwatching checklist.
about_urban_p2: The idea (borrowing from R. Murray Schafer's soundscape ecology and from bioacoustic monitoring used in conservation) is to log where and when species are heard, not just identify them once. Enough logged points over time could start to show things a map of buildings alone won't: which green corridors still connect, which courtyards and parks function as real habitat versus decorative landscaping, and how that shifts as construction density increases along the coast.
about_urban_p4: The app also logs a rough, relative sound-level reading alongside bird detections (reusing the same microphone analysis, no extra hardware) — the two together start to look like the classic urban-soundscape pairing of noise pollution vs. biodiversity, rather than a birding checklist. This reading is not a calibrated SPL/dB(A) meter — phone and browser microphones have unknown, varying gain — so treat it as relative (louder/quieter, here vs. there) rather than an absolute decibel figure.
about_urban_p3: This is a hobby project, not a peer-reviewed study — treat any pattern it turns up as a lead to investigate, not a finding.
about_roadmap_p1: Right now every detection stays on your own device. The next step under consideration is a shared map: after a detection you confirm, it gets pinned at your location so a community map of "who's singing where" builds up over time — the kind of citizen-science layer bird atlases and eBird already do at country scale, just local and passive.
about_roadmap_p2: The likely path is a Telegram Mini App rather than a separate native app: it's just this same web app opened inside Telegram, so the detection code doesn't change. Telegram already identifies the user to the app with a small signed payload, no separate login screen needed — sharing a location pin is one tap, not an account. The only new piece is a small shared backend to store and serve the pins on a map.
about_birdnet_p1: BirdNET is a research platform developed jointly by the K. Lisa Yang Center for Conservation Bioacoustics at the Cornell Lab of Ornithology and the Chemnitz University of Technology.
about_birdnet_p2: The project uses artificial neural networks to train computers to identify more than 6,000 of the most common bird species worldwide. It is one of the most advanced tools for acoustic monitoring of birds.
about_step1_desc: Your browser's Web Audio API captures sound from your microphone and resamples it to 48,000 Hz.
about_step2_desc: The audio is sliced into 3-second chunks. These chunks are converted into Mel Spectrograms—visual representations of sound that show frequency intensity over time.
about_step3_desc: The spectrogram image is fed into a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). This model has learned to "see" bird calls in these images, distinguishing them from noise and other animals.
about_step4_desc: The model outputs probability scores for thousands of species. We apply a confidence threshold (e.g., 25%) and optionally a location filter to hide birds that are unlikely to be found in your area.
about_contact_desc: Questions or feedback about this project (not the BirdNET research itself — see the official site above for that):
about_github_desc: The source code for Sochi Birds is available on GitHub, forked from BirdNET Live. Feel free to inspect the code, report issues, or contribute improvements!
share_desc: Scan this code to open the app on another device.
share_msg_text: Check out this real-time bird ID app!
